coredata.dc:title: Preparation and characterization of quantum dot doped polyaniline photoactive film for organic solar cell application
coredata.dc:creator: Shanmugasundaram, Esakkimuthu
Ganesan, Vigneshkumar
Narayanan, Vimalasruthi
Perumalsamy, Muthukumar
Kuppu, Sakthi Velu
Guruviah, Paruthimal Kalaignan
Thambusamy, Stalin
coredata.dc:description: Upconversion materials play a significant role in different applications like bio-imaging, solar cells, and therapy treatment due to their light conversion behavior (lower energy photon to higher energy photon). This material is a doping material for the improvement of the light-harvesting properties of polymers. However, there are limited publications in this field. Deposited/fabricated upconversion carbon quantum dot doped polyaniline photoactive film by electropolymerization. First, we prepared the quantum dot from the source of ascorbic acid and studied the quantum dot optical and structural properties by ultraviolet(UV), fluorescence (FL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), spectroscopy. The quantum dot is doped with polyaniline (PANI) using the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) plate for making a film for this research. The prepared film has been analyzed spectroscopically for photophysical properties (absorption, transmission, band gap), surface morphology, and thermal stability of this merit to the solar cell. The current density and impedance spectra are have well supported by such a material characterization that might be used for the application of organic solar cells. Finally, the Förster resonance energy transfer of the quantum dot (donor) to the polyaniline (acceptor) is confirmed. With the help of the above characterization, results are supporting the organic solar cell.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Upconversion
Electropolymerization
Photoactive film
Förster resonance energy transfer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of PANI photoactive film by electropolymerization
Conformational characterization of carbon quantum dot
Fluorescence behavior of carbon quantum dot
Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectra analysis
Study of thermal and XRD patterns

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–Visible spectra [insect Fig. 1.CQD in normal light, 2.CQD in UV light(360 nm)], (B) FT-IR spectra and (C) TEM image of Carbon Quantum dot.
Fluorescence spectra of CQD in different excitation wavelength (290–450 nm).
Upconversion spectra of CQD in different wavelength.
(A) Electropolymerization of polyaniline at 20 mV/s in 10scans (B) 2nd scan of polyaniline from (A) graph.
(A) UV–Visible, (B) Transmittance spectra and (C) Fluorescence spectra of PANI, PANI − 0.5 mL CQD, PANI − 1.0 mL CQD.
(a) Bandgap graph of PANI, (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD, (c) PANI − 1.0 mL CQD.
(A) FT-IR spectra and (B) Raman spectra of (a) PANI (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD (c) PANI − 1.0 mL CQD.
(A). (a) SEM image of PANI (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD (c) PANI − 1 mL CQD and Fig. 9 (B). (a) Roughness calculation of PANI (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD (c) PANI − 1 mL CQD.
(a) TGA thermogram of PANI (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD (c) PANI − 1 mL CQD.
(a) XRD pattern of PANI (b) PANI − 0.5 mL CQD (c) PANI − 1 mL CQD.
(A) Cyclic voltammetry study and (B) Impedance spectra of PANI, PANI − 0.5 mL CQD, PANI − 1.0 mL CQD in 1 M H2SO4.

head.ce:title: Preparation and characterization of quantum dot doped polyaniline photoactive film for organic solar cell application

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630 003, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630 003, Tamil Nadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Upconversion materials play a significant role in different applications like bio-imaging, solar cells, and therapy treatment due to their light conversion behavior (lower energy photon to higher energy photon). This material is a doping material for the improvement of the light-harvesting properties of polymers. However, there are limited publications in this field. Deposited/fabricated upconversion carbon quantum dot doped polyaniline photoactive film by electropolymerization. First, we prepared the quantum dot from the source of ascorbic acid and studied the quantum dot optical and structural properties by ultraviolet(UV), fluorescence (FL), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR), spectroscopy. The quantum dot is doped with polyaniline (PANI) using the fluorine-doped tin oxide (FTO) plate for making a film for this research. The prepared film has been analyzed spectroscopically for photophysical properties (absorption, transmission, band gap), surface morphology, and thermal stability of this merit to the solar cell. The current density and impedance spectra are have well supported by such a material characterization that might be used for the application of organic solar cells. Finally, the Förster resonance energy transfer of the quantum dot (donor) to the polyaniline (acceptor) is confirmed. With the help of the above characterization, results are supporting the organic solar cell.

ce:section.ce:para: In this modern world, electricity demand is a terrific issue that the reason is uncontrollable population growth so that we try to improve electricity production in different ways. Renewable and non-renewable sources are commonly using for electricity generation [1]. Non-renewable like coal, oil, nuclear and natural gases are limited sources and take a long-time refill. Renewable such as wind, water, solar, and biomass are unlimited sources and easily refill themselves so that this is a promising source for future electricity production [2]. Compare to all renewable sources, one of the most abundant renewable energy sources globally that energy converts to electricity with the help of photovoltaic cells [3]. Light-harvesting organic semiconductors like conducting polymers are using this sunlight energy and produce electricity. Conducting polymers such as polyaniline [4], polypyrrole [5], and polythiophene [6,7] play an essential role in the light-harvesting process for organic photovoltaic cells due to their optical and electrical properties. Most of the research papers published based on functionalized polythiophene moiety as a light-harvesting donor material due to their low bandgap structure, quickly absorb the solar radiation, and release the electron to the acceptor molecule [8]. Compared to polythiophene, polyaniline is one of the most often using polymers in different energy applications [9,10], mainly used as a hole transfer layer in perovskite [11] and organic solar cell [12] applications due to their unique properties like environmental stability, low-cost, facile synthesis, and easy doping method [13]. However, this is not suitable for the light-harvesting layer in organic solar cells because its bandgap only matches the visible light region.

Evan A. Gizzie et al. reported that the polyaniline (PANI) acts as donor material, absorption at 633 nm (visible light) used in biohybrid energy conversion device [14]. Also, the solar cells were investigated with some doped polyaniline used as the hole transport layer [15] and exhibited a much more stable performance regarding the efficiency of the polyaniline:graphene-oxide materials [16]. These results confirm that PANI is a suitable material for visible light harvesting. The earth receives a broad range of (52–55%) infrared radiation (low energy above 700 nm) from the sun, but PANI cannot fully absorb this infrared radiation since its mismatch bandgap behavior. Therefore, we must be doping the polymers with light conversion (infrared to visible radiation) material or photosensitizer to use this type of polymers in sunlight harvesting. The conversion of infrared light (lower energy) to visible light (higher) is called upconversion (UC) [17], which material involved in this process is known as UC materials. Typically, organic and inorganic materials are using for the UC process. In this process, the material absorbs the higher-energy photons. The electrons exit from highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO); then, it emits the lower energy photons to the return process LUMO to HOMO. This releasing photon energy transfer from one material to another material is called Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [18]. Our research group published papers based on the FRET process in the sensor field [19–21]. Lanthanide-based materials [22] LaF3: Yb3 +/Er3+, [23] NaYF4: Er3+ and organic based materials naphtho-p-quinodimethane (QDM) with perylene [24] have UC properties, so they are suitable materials for light harvesting in organic solar cell applications.

Compared to other UC materials, carbon quantum dot (CQD) has unique properties such as cost-effective, easy synthesis, less toxicity, good solubility, and biocompatibility. Recent publications proved that CQD also has UC properties due to its broad absorption and emission behavior. Raju Kahn et al. reported that starch source-based CQD is a good near infrared spectroscopy (NIR) UC material for sensing glutathione [25]. Some researchers currently demonstrated that 2-azido imidazole [26], graphene hydrazine hydrate [27], and cabbage sources [28] of CQD are applicable in bioimaging, sensing, and pharmaceutical applications. One-pot pH-sensitive CQD synthesized from ascorbic acid for biomedical applications [29] and source with TiO2 is used as a photocatalyst for dye degradation at visible light. In this process, the CQD absorbs infrared (IR) from the sunlight [30]. These reports suggested that the CQD is an energetic UC material due to the conversion property of IR to visible. We hope that if the material doping with conducting polymers, it can reduce the bandgap mismatch problem and improve organic solar cell efficiency. So, we prepare the PANI photoactive film from aniline monomer with CQD.

There are two ways to aniline convert to PANI, such as chemical polymerization and electropolymerization. Among these methods, electropolymerization is the best way to polymerize for solar cell applications. In our group reported papers in dye sensitization solar cells [31–34] based on electropolymerization. This method is no catalyst, so this is eco-friendly, the current (I) used as a catalyst. Usually, spin coating is a method for donor material deposits on the fluorine tin oxide (FTO) plate in the organic solar cell. However, this method has some troubles like polymers insoluble in some solvents, low binding strength, and low stability. The electropolymerization method tackles this problem because, in this setup, we use a monomer solution to convert to polymers, and deposit on the FTO plate is used as a donor material in solar cells[35]. Moreover, the polymer layer depends upon the scan rate, so control polymer growth on the FTO plate.

The electropolymerization method is used to produce a PANI polymer based photoactive film. From aniline monomer with CQD solution reported in this work. First, we prepare three polymer-coated FTO plates as undoped PANI, 0.5 mL, and 1 mL CQD doped PANI (PANI − 0.5 mL CQD, and PANI − 1.0 mL CQD). Typically, the PANI plate has no much more light-harvesting properties due to the high bandgap. But CQD doped PANI plates are high absorption and involve an energy transfer mechanism, which is confirming with the help of ultraviolet (UV) and fluorescence (FL) studies. When the sunlight interrupts the FTO plate, the CQD is stimulating. The electron transfer from HOMO to LUMO is returning to the normal state, emits the visible light (400–600 nm) that radiation is suitable for PANI excitation. The visible light quickly transfers to the polymers called FRET, evaluated by FL spectra. The polymers used this light for electron excitation and the electron transfer to the acceptor layer, then the electron extract with the help of cathode and passing away to the outer circuit and produces the electricity. Therefore, these materials may apply to the organic solar cell application.

L-ascorbic acid, ethanol, aniline, sulfuric acid, fluorine doped tin oxide (FTO) plates were from Sigma Aldrich chemical limited. All chemicals are analytical grade, which has been using without any further purification. Before using, aniline was double distilled.

2 g of L-ascorbic acid dissolved in 30 mL of water and ethanol (1:1) was adding to this solution, which was stirring until the colloidal solution changed to a transparent solution. Then, the solution transferred to the teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave was maintained at 180 °C for 6 h and cooled at room temperature (RT). Finally, a brown solution has obtaining that was treated and extracted with dichloromethane, and the aqueous medium has also separated. For two days, this is dialyzing to remove impurities by a dialysis membrane[30]. After dialysis, the yellow color clear solution obtained that was stored and used in the experimental studies (Fig. S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PANI photoactive film by electropolymerization
ce:section.ce:para: The photoactive film has prepared with the help of three electrodes-based electrochemical instruments. In this setup, FTO is using as a working electrode (WE), silver/silver chloride used as a reference electrode (RE), and platinum is as a counter electrode (CE). 0.1 M aniline solution dissolves in 1 M H2SO4, which acts as an electrolyte. Before polymerization, the FTO plate is cleaning with acetone, ethanol, and distilled water. The polymerization has carried out by a potentiostat method [36] in which the window setup is −0.8 to + 1.8, and the scan rate is 20 mV/s with 10 scans. After polymerization, the polymers-coated electrode is dipped in 0.1 M H2SO4 to remove the monomers and lose binding polymers from the plate. Finally, this is to dry at RT. The same procedure followed to prepare the PANI - CQD, but in this stage, we added 0.5 mL and 1 mL of CQD solution in the aniline electrolyte solution.

Ultraviolet (UV) spectroscopy (Jasco V530 UV Visible spectrometer) has to record CQD's absorbance in the wavelength range of 200 to 800 nm. The emission and upconversion properties of CQD evaluate from the fluorescence spectra (JASCO fluorometer). Functional group identification was by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy in the wavenumber range 400–4000 cm−1. The surface morphology is of the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) image was exhibited by using JEOL 3010 transmission electron microscopy operating voltage 300 kV. The electropolymerization and electrochemical studies did by metrohm electrochemical workstation in a three-electrode system such as the working electrode (WE), the counter electrode (CE), and the reference electrode (RE). 1 M H2SO4 is using as an electrolyte in the electrochemical current (cyclic voltammetry) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy study (CV & EIS, Autolab, PGSTAT30). Absorption, transmittance, and bandgap of polymer-coated FTO plates are recording by ultraviolet (UV) spectrometer (Jasco V530 UV Visible spectrometer). Upconversion and fluorescence studies have carried out using the JASCO fluorometer. The Raman spectroscopy (STR 500 mm focal length laser) is recording for functional group identification. The morphology and average roughness (AR) of plates were analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, VEGA 3 TESCAN) and ImageJ software package, respectively. The plate thermal stability is have measured by thermogravimetric analysis (TGA, STA 2500 regulus simultaneous thermal analyzer) temperature 100–6000 °C (200C/min) in a nitrogen atmosphere.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conformational characterization of carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 shows the optical, vibrational, and structural properties of carbon quantum dot (CQD) derived from ascorbic acid. Fig. 1(A) shows two absorption peaks presented at 273 nm and 321 nm, which ascribed the π-π* transition of C = C bond and n-π* transition of C = O bond, respectively. Furthermore, Fig. 1 (A) inset figure shows the naked eye fluorescence behavior of the quantum dot. The solution is yellow in visible light. It emits pink color when it places in the ultraviolet(UV) chamber and passes UV rays at 360 nm and the solution is glinting which proves that the quantum dot has a strong fluorescence nature at 360 nm. The FT-IR respective peaks are presented in Fig. 1(B), that peaks confirm the ascorbic acid source is present in the CQD [37]. It is well-known that ascorbic acid has four functional groups, as O-H, C = O, C-O, and = C-H. The U-shape peak at 3302 cm−1 indicates the O-H stretching vibration, and the respective peaks observed at 1653 cm−1 refer to the carboxyl group (C = O) stretch frequency. The C = C Peak presented at 1266 cm−1, whereas the = C-H peak occurred at 681 cm−1. These peaks demonstrated that the CQD has a hydrophilic nature. Moreover, this CQD easily binds to other materials due to the O-H, C = O functional groups present in the outer surface. Fig. 1(C) displays the particle size and TEM morphology image of the CQD. It shows the spherical structure shapes and the particle size between 6 and 10 nm. The inset picture described the lattice figure structure, which is confirmed the quantum dot crystalline property.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence behavior of carbon quantum dot
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 displays the fluorescence spectra of a quantum dot in different excitation wavelengths (290–450 nm). The fluorescence intensity is increased from 290 nm to 360 nm excitation and then gradually decreased. The high emission intensity at 450 nm was observed at 360 nm excitation. The reason for the emission wavelength shifting and intensity change is the various size and structures (sp2, sp3) of carbon quantum dot (CQD). The reason for the fluorescence behaviour of CQD is maybe the carbon spheres energy release and quantum confinement.

The upconversion (UC) property of CQD has recorded by fluorescence spectra shown in Fig. 3 . The CQD solution is excited from the visible region of 600 to 750 nm. There is no peak at 600 nm. When the excitation range is increasing, the fluorescence intensity also increased. At 750 nm, at the long ultraviolet–visible range of 450–500 nm is observed. The spectra reached a high intensity, confirming this CQD has a predicted UC property. This property is an excellent tool for light-harvesting in organic solar cell applications. Mostly, conducting polymers are using in the organic solar cell due to their semiconductor behavior. However, the efficiency is low because the bandgap is a mismatch to the solar spectrum so that the polymers cannot observe the whole solar energy. If polymers doped some photosensitized materials, it will easily convert the light energy to electrical energy [38]. This CQD material does the work properly because it receives the sunlight energy and emits UV rays that ray suitable for polymers electron excitation. Therefore, polymers blend CQD is the best light-harvesting material in organic solar cell devices. In this work, we doped this CQD with PANI for light-harvesting material in organic solar cell applications.

The energy level diagram in Fig. 4 clearly explains the upconversion property of carbon quantum dots. The fluorescence spectrum is based on the lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) transition to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO), which was shown in Fig. 4 The size of the quantum dot decides the energy bandgap. It is well-known that the bandgap of the ascorbic acid is 5 eV. When the bandgap is decreased, the size of the quantum dot is increased. The different sizes of the quantum dot present in the synthesized CQD was confirmed by HR-TEM and fluorescence spectroscopy (different size quantum dot have different fluorescence behavior). The upconversion quantum dot is an anti-stoke transition. It has two ground state multiplicity ϭ and π orbitals. The ‘E’ is the energy difference between the σ and π orbitals. Hoffmann proposed the energy difference (E) should be below 1.5 ev. In this work, the energy different between the excitation and the emission light is Em = 1.00Ex + δ E (R2 = 0.96674) with δE = 0.99 eV. When the lower energy photons excite the electron from the π orbital, the electron transfer to the lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO), then the electron come back to the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) which emits the unconverted (low energy to higher energy) light. Even though the electrons of the σ orbital also transmitted, it only emits normal fluorescence. This mechanism proves that the synthesized carbon quantum dot has upconversion property.

Electropolymerisation of polyaniline (PANI) with CQD showed in Fig. 5 (A). This experiment was run by the potentiostatic method in cyclic voltammetry (CV) that the scan rate is 20 mV/s with 10 scans. There is no proper oxidation and reduction in the first scan because the electrode starts the attraction with the monomer solution because of that scan time. When the second scan is starting, four types of different peaks present in the graph. This graph is predicting in Fig. 5(B). Generally, aniline's polymerization has three steps, such as leucoemeraldine, emeraldine salt, and pernigraniline. In the first step, the leucoemeraldine base oxidized and convert to emeraldine salt; that time, the color changed from colorless to green on the FTO surface in the CV solution. Then, the plate color changed green to light blue, which indicates the conversion of emeraldine salt to pernigraniline. These two steps have occurred in the upper graph because which is the oxidation process. In the next step, pernigraniline converts to emeraldine, and the base convert to leucoemeraldine. This step is called the reduction process. In the oxidization step, electrons are losing, and in the reduction step, electrons are gain so that this reaction is a redox reaction [39]. This electron source converts the monomer to the polymers with the help of sulfuric acid and applied potential. The process had continued after 10 cycles, the thin film coated on the FTO surface, which is confirmed by this layer (Thin) graphs (Fig. 5A) of CV.

The absorption spectra of FTO coated films are showing in Fig. 6 (A). PANI film's first absorption peak presented at 325 nm is the π-π* transition of PANI, whereas the other two film peaks are slightly red-shifted. This increment of redshift depends on the CQD doping level in the film. PANI – 0.5 mL CQD film has a small bathochromic shift, which is 5 nm (325–330 nm), but the PANI − 1.0 mL CQD film has a higher bathochromic shifting observed is 49 nm. Moreover, the three films have another peak at 631, 643 and 668 nm, respectively, that peak confirms the polaron and bipolaron states in the PANI chains. The same redshift also appeared in the visible region (400–700 nm). The result demonstrates that compared to the undoped film, doped films have a higher absorption capacity. The transmittance spectra of FTO coated films displays in Fig. 6(B). It is well-known that transmittance is one of the critical factors of solar cell application [40]. When transmittance is decreasing, the absorption behavior automatically increased. Therefore, low transmittance material plays a vital role in light-harvesting. In this study, the transmittance behavior had fallen in the visible region when the CQD's doping level had increased. PANI − 1.0 mL CQD film has low transmittance in these visible regions, whereas its transmittance behavior is very high in the UV region. This result proves that this material absorbs visible light and transmits or filters the UV light. This study suggests that doped PANI film is a suitable sun light-harvesting material for organic solar cells.

Un-doped and doped films are excited at 350 nm in FL spectroscopy that data displayed in Fig. 6(C). PANI film has no high intensity, but it's reaching a higher range of PANI – 0.5 mL CQD film fluorescence intensity. However, PANI − 1.0 mL CQD also has fluorescence behavior; as well as emission intensities are also slightly decreasing. The reason is that the level of the CQD increasing in the film, the quantum dot emitting fluorescence light is quenching by the polymer. Due to this binding strength between the CQD and polymer. In this case, the CQD acts as a donor, and the polymer acts as an acceptor [41]. That the energy transition from donor to acceptor [42] is called FRET. This overall result clarifies that the CQD binds to the polymer matrix and the film involves FRET.

This formula/Eq. (1) measures the band gap value for doped and undoped PANI film.
(1)
α
h υ
= A ∗
h υ - E g
n / 2
→ equation

α is the absorption coefficient, h is Planck's constant, υ is light frequency, A is the constant's value, and Eg is bandgap energy, and n is the number of transitions in the semiconductor[43]. The bandgap is decreasing when the CQD level increases in the film due to the semiconductor property. The bandgap of PANI, PANI – 0.5 mL CQD, and PANI-1 mL CQD is 2.616 eV, 2.601 eV, and 2.592 eV, respectively which is shown in Fig. 7 . The low bandgap doped PANI is a suitable material for organic solar cells. Because the lowering bandgap materials only easily observe the longer wavelength light and involve excitation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fourier transform infrared and Raman spectra analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 8 (A) displays the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of doped and undoped PANI films. (a) spectra line denoted the undoped PANI film stretching and bending vibrations. ‘V’ shaped peak at 3290 cm−1 is ascribing to the stretching vibration N-H. The nearly same frequency range of peak value has been presenting in (b) and (c) spectra. The peak at 2980 and 2853 cm−1 indicates symmetric and asymmetric stretching of C-H. N-H and C = C (quinoid ring) stretching, C-N-stretching, (N = Q = N) (Q: quinoid ring) are presenting at 1645, 1257, and 885 cm−1, respectively. The six functional group peaks are also showing in the (b) and (c), but the range is slighted change due to PANI and CQD interaction. For instance, (c) spectra C-H asymmetry and symmetry stretching in the ranging at 2922 and 2853 cm−1, which is a strong intensity peak than (a) spectra. Moreover, the C-O stretching frequency presented at 1300 and 1465 cm−1 in (b) and (c) confirms that the CQD present in the PANI film.

The Raman spectrum shows the undoped and doped PANI chains structure in Fig. 8(B). The undoped PANI [44] spectrum show peaks at 1151, 1344, and1598 cm−1 corresponding to C-H bending (Quinoid ring), C-N Stretching (bipoloraon), N-H bending, respectively. The same three peaks are also presenting in doped films, but these peaks' values have slightly shifted the position, which is denoting that the PANI quinoid ring forms a strong π – π interaction to the CQD [45].

Fig. 9 (A) and Fig. 9(B) demonstrating the surface morphology and average roughness diameter (ARD) of three PANI coated plates measured by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-SEM) and roughness calculator. The PANI plate has a chain-like structure, denoted that the PANI chain grows on the FTO plate [46], and the ARD is 261 nm. The PANI − 0.5 mL CQD plate surface roughness is 269 nm that is slightly higher than PANI. The polymer growth is also equally spread on the FTO plate due to PANI and CQD interaction. Compared to others, the PANI − 1 mL CQD plate has a high ARD 291 nm, and the bulk chain structure confirms the CQD strongly binds to the PANI Chain.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of thermal and XRD patterns
ce:section.ce:para: The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) thermogram study of polymer-coated plates is depicting in Fig. 10 . The water and moisture decomposition appear before 100 °C so that, we start the compound decomposition analysis at 100 °C. Typically, the PANI chain decomposition is two stages as 200–300 °C and more than 500 °C, which correspond to the elimination of acid and the N-Phenylaniline, methane, and carbazole decomposition in the polymer chain [47]. The CQD doped polymer plate decomposition is also present in the same range, but the slopes are slightly bending than undoped polymers. This data confirms that the doping polymers have higher weight loss than undoped polymers because the reason is the CQD molecules oxidation and thermal decomposition due to the forming residue oxide during the test [48]. The result proves that the CQD bind with the polymer chain.

Crystalline conducting polymers have more attractive in the field of solar cells due to their superior hole mobility. The X-ray pattern shown in Fig. 11 , shows that the PANI film has the crystalline phase of its emeraldine salt form. The peak is located at 2θ = 27.58°, created from the Braggs reflection, and the Millar plane is 020. The peak intensity of PANI is increased based on the level of CQD doping. Compared to PANI, PANI + 1 mL CQD peak intensity is high because the CQD acts as a surfactant, so that it improves the crystalline polymer growth on the FTO substrate. The other three peaks at 2θ = 34.84°, 38.64° and 52.40°, are the FTO glass substrate's corresponded peaks.

Fig. 12 (A) exhibits the FTO coated polymers are characterized by potentiostatic in a cyclic voltammetry (CV) at 5 mV/s in 1 M H2SO4. The PANI current range is 3.8 mV, and the current density range is minimum. PANI − 0.5 mL CQD film current range is 4.4 mV due to the CQD semiconductivity nature. Furthermore, PANI − 1.0 mL CQD current range is 5.8 mV, and the current density also increased. Fig. 12(B) shows the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) spectra of photoactive films. EIS analysis is a potential method [49] for analysis of the electrode and electrolyte interfaces. We analyze three different types of electrodes in 1 M H2SO4. The Rct (Charge transfer resistance) value of PANI, PANI − 0.5 mL CQD and PANI − 1.0 mL CQD is 22.35 Ω cm2, 9.68 Ω cm2, 2.69 Ω cm2, respectively. The results suggested that the PANI − 1.0 mL CQD has higher conductivity [50] and proves the binding strength of CQD semiconductor and polymers.

PANI photoactive films having prepared from the CQD with aniline monomer using mild electrochemical polymerization. This type of polymerization is a rapid assemble process so that the carbon dots strong integrate into the PANI conjugate chain. As a result, the significant enhancement in absorbance and the lower transmittance presented in the visible region. The high current range and fluorescence measurements confirm the FRET between the CQD and polymer chains. Moreover, the current density and lower resistance value are better from CV spectra. Typically, the organic solar cell donor material must have some properties such as high absorption, low transmittance, shorter bandgap, and higher current density. Our polymer film has all properties so that these results suggest that this film is a suitable and low-cost material for organic solar cell applications.

Esakkimuthu Shanmugasundaram: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Vigneshkumar Ganesan: Resources, Data curation. Vimalasruthi Narayanan: Formal analysis, Data curation. Muthukumar Perumalsamy: Validation. Sakthi Velu Kuppu: Validation. Paruthimal Kalaignan Guruviah: Validation. Stalin Thambusamy: Supervision, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Dr. Stalin, thank you very much to the Science and Engineering Research Board (SERB), Government of India. The paperwork funded by SERB File No.: EEQ/2018/001455.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2021.138517.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Review of electrical energy storage technologies, materials and systems: Challenges and prospects for large-scale grid storage
Energy Environ. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.M. Gür, Review of electrical energy storage technologies, materials and systems: Challenges and prospects for large-scale grid storage, Energy Environ. Sci. 11 (2018) 2696-2767.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lithium-doped conjugated microporous polymers for reversible hydrogen storage
Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Li, R.F. Lu, Y. Wang, X. Wang, K.L. Han, W.Q. Deng, Lithium-doped conjugated microporous polymers for reversible hydrogen storage, Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed. 49 (2010) 3330-3333

Chem. Phys. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: X. Xie, Z. Liu, W. Li, F.Q. Bai, E.C. Lee, H.X. Zhang, Theoretical study on organic dyes with tunable Π-spacers for dye-sensitized solar cells: Inspired by the organic polymer photovoltaics, Chem. Phys. Lett. 719 (2019) 39-44.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: S. ul Haque, Inamuddin, A. Nasar, A.M. Asiri, Fabrication and characterization of electrochemically prepared bioanode (polyaniline/ferritin/glucose oxidase) for biofuel cell application, Chem. Phys. Lett. 692 (2018) 277-284.

RSC Adv.

Chem. Soc. Rev.
